stem: Pipe A fills a storage tank at a rate of 15 gallons per minute. Pipe B fills the same tank at a rate of 9 gallons per minute. If both pipes operate simultaneously and the tank holds 360 gallons, how many seconds will it take to completely fill the empty tank?
explanation:
Step 1: Find the combined fill rate of both pipes. $$15 + 9 = 24 \text{ gallons per minute}$$

Step 2: Find the time in minutes to fill the 360-gallon tank. $$\text{Time} = \frac{360}{24} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

Step 3: Convert minutes to seconds. $$15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ seconds}$$

The tank will be completely filled in 900 seconds.

distractorRationale.A: Stopped at intermediate value — the student correctly combines the rates ($15 + 9 = 24$ gal/min) and computes the fill time ($360 \div 24 = 15$ minutes), but reports 15 as the final answer without converting to seconds.
distractorRationale.B: Correct answer. Combined rate = 24 gal/min; time = $360 \div 24 = 15$ min; $15 \times 60 = 900$ seconds.
distractorRationale.C: Rate applied to wrong quantity — the student ignores Pipe B and uses only Pipe A's rate to fill the tank, then converts to seconds. Wrong calculation: $360 \div 15 = 24$ minutes; $24 \times 60 = 1{,}440$ seconds.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong unit conversion factor — the student correctly finds 15 minutes but converts to seconds using a factor of 120 instead of 60 (perhaps confusing seconds per minute with some other factor). Wrong calculation: $15 \times 120 = 1{,}800$ seconds.
